step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("light cure composite polymerization duration")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

Based on the detailed information gathered about light-cure composite polymers, none of the listed options appear fundamentally flawed regarding standard practices unless considering how they might apply across all types of dental composites generally vs. specific nuances tied to particular brands/models/formulations. Option D ("Adding increments of 1-2 mm") aligns well with typical guidelines ensuring adequate exposure throughout each increment placed. Options B & A relate correctly to necessary safeguards protecting operators/patients from harmful emissions. That leaves option C asserting continuous polymerization beyond initial irradiation—a common misunderstanding arising because once activated, subsequent prolonged exposure doesn't enhance already-complete curing processes; instead, it risks overheating/degrading material integrity unnecessarily. Therefore, my stance remains consistent: \boxed{C} disagrees since it misrepresents actual behavior of cured composites after initial activation.
Answer Choice:

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

Based on the detailed information gathered about light-cure composite polymers, none of the listed options appear fundamentally flawed regarding standard practices unless considering how they might apply across all types of dental composites generally vs. specific nuances tied to particular brands/models/formulations. Option D ("Adding increments of 1-2 mm") aligns well with typical guidelines ensuring adequate exposure throughout each increment placed. Options B & A relate correctly to necessary safeguards protecting operators/patients from harmful emissions. That leaves option C asserting continuous polymerization beyond initial irradiation—a common misunderstanding arising because once activated, subsequent prolonged exposure doesn't enhance already-complete curing processes; instead, it risks overheating/degrading material integrity unnecessarily. Therefore, my stance remains consistent: \boxed{C} disagrees since it misrepresents actual behavior of cured composites after initial activation.
Answer Choice: \boxed{C}. Disagree - Continuous polymerization continuing up to 72 hours is not supported by evidence showing completion soon after illumination ceases.
